name: Prague
description:
Prague, the capital of the Czech Republic, is one of Europe's most beautiful and well-preserved cities. Known as the City of a Hundred Spires, Prague's skyline is punctuated by Gothic towers, Baroque domes, and Art Nouveau facades that reflect over a thousand years of architectural history. Unlike many European capitals, Prague survived World War II largely undamaged, preserving its medieval, Renaissance, and Baroque heritage intact.

Prague Castle (Prazsky hrad), perched on a hill above the Vltava River, is the largest ancient castle complex in the world, covering over 70,000 square meters. Within its walls stand St. Vitus Cathedral, a stunning Gothic masterpiece that took nearly 600 years to complete, the Old Royal Palace, and the charming Golden Lane with its tiny, colorful houses where Franz Kafka once lived.

Charles Bridge (Karluv most), built in 1357, connects the castle district to the Old Town across the Vltava River. Lined with 30 Baroque statues of saints, the bridge is a favorite gathering spot for artists, musicians, and tourists. The Old Town Square features the famous Astronomical Clock (Orloj), installed in 1410, which performs a mechanical show of apostles every hour and is the oldest working astronomical clock in the world.

Czech cuisine is hearty and satisfying, with dishes designed for the country's cold winters. Traditional meals include svickova (marinated beef sirloin with creamy sauce and dumplings), vepro-knedlo-zelo (roast pork with sauerkraut and dumplings), and trdelnik (chimney cake pastry rolled in sugar and cinnamon). Czech beer culture is unmatched — the country has the highest per-capita beer consumption in the world, and Prague's beer halls serve excellent pilsners and craft beers at remarkably affordable prices.

The city's cultural scene is thriving, with world-class opera at the National Theatre, classical concerts in historic churches, and a vibrant contemporary art scene in neighborhoods like Holesovice and Karlin.
best_time: April to June, September to October
fun_fact: Prague's Astronomical Clock has been operating since 1410, making it the oldest working astronomical clock in the world.
